2064682482876747861.text:
#KostasThoughts: Someone recently asked me about automating slide creation.  For me, creating slides is part of the learning process.  Breaking a topic into its building blocks and putting it back together helps me understand it.

I hope my students learn from the lecture, but I learn a great deal through the creation (and presenting) process as well.

That said, I have started using automated tools for ideation, evaluating my slide compositions, and generating images and videos.

TLDR: If I don’t understand it, I won’t present it.

2064009179539427834.text:
#KostasThoughts: I’ve been thinking about the criteria for award-winning papers 🏆 They should embody what our community values most: originality, potential impact, and reproducibility. Yet (full) reproducibility is often missing, even among award-winning papers.

Making code and model weights publicly available should be a criterion for award consideration. This would send a strong signal to decision-makers in both academia and industry about the importance of open and reproducible research.

Papers would receive the recognition they deserve, while the community would gain a deeper understanding of the engineering innovations that drive performance.

Thoughts?

2063884678700945896.text: #KostasThoughts : If a paper promises code/weights in the review submission, then it must be made available by the time of the conference; otherwise, conference should desk reject the paper.

2055257932955009052.text:
#KostasThoughts: While consensus among reviewers is appreciated, it is not strictly necessary. As an Area Chair, what matters most is understanding each reviewer’s reasoning and decision-making process. That is why it is imperative that reviewers provide a clear final summary explaining why they recommend accepting or rejecting the paper, which concerns were addressed through the rebuttal and discussion, and which concerns, if any, remain unresolved.  Simply putting “my concerns were not resolved” is not being a responsible reviewer.

If you initially misunderstood parts of the paper or made an error in your assessment, that’s okay we all make mistakes. Please put personal ego aside and focus on making the correct decision based on the technical merits of the paper, the rebuttal, and the discussion.

2044507813414588654.text: #KostasThoughts : Perhaps we’ve gone too far with requiring ALL authors to be in the reviewer pool in computer vision conferences and instead we should consider a per paper nomination system 🤔

Does speed trump depth? 🤔

Great podcast on this rapidly evolving shift.

#KostasThoughts: Like many others, I used to cram too many images or videos onto one slide.  They end up too small, the audience doesn’t know where to look, and you rush through them.  My rule now: one idea or example per slide.  Slides are free, use them.

Here's a comparison: (left) cramming examples, and (right) letting each example breathe on its own slide.  

Which do you prefer?

1994443941421195411.text:
#KostasThoughts: The #ICLR2026 review process has been disappointing. This is not on the PCs. It reflects our current environment where the scale of reviewing across the various conferences may have outgrown the bandwidth of the community (eg shortcuts via LLM generated reviews) and where some feel pressured enough to lose sight of basic ethics (eg reports of authors contacting reviewers to change their score or much worse if social media reports are to be believed).

Perhaps it is time to rethink our conference culture.

Share your thoughts in thread.

1991929937020830002.text:
#KostasThoughts: A prof’s email is not an admissions portal! Many prospective students copy-and-paste their statement of purpose or use a generic template (sometimes LLM-generated) and add a line or two about the prof’s broad research area and random paper from the professor’s website. Emails like this rarely get attention.

Instead, keep the email short, personalised, and focused; attach your CV.  Show how your background and goals genuinely connect to the prof’s current research trajectory.  Personalizing the email is important if you want to improve your chances.

The email is meant to be an icebreaker. If the prof is actively looking for students and their interest is piqued, they will reply.

Always begin formally: “Dear Professor <Last name>.” All my students, including undergrads, call me “Kosta”, but not every prof prefers that. Stay formal until invited otherwise.

Good luck!

1990236020881908038.text: #KostasThoughts : Please stop using AI tools to write reviews. I want your authentic thoughts, even if they contain spelling, grammar, & factual mistakes. An LLM should not influence your judgment. The purpose of multiple reviewers is to provide diverse views and error checking.
1990236020881908038.html:
#KostasThoughts: Please stop using AI tools to write reviews. I want your authentic thoughts, even if they contain spelling, grammar, & factual mistakes. An LLM should not influence your judgment. The purpose of multiple reviewers is to provide diverse views and error checking.

1922401234348073114.text:
#KostasThoughts:  A common issue I see in the “related work” section of many papers is that they list similar prior work without clearly contextualizing their own contributions.  The goal of this section is not just to cite related methods, but to compare and contrast them, highlighting how your approach differs and what advantages it offers.

Before complaining that the reviewers didn’t understand your contributions, take another look at your related work section, that’s where the disconnect happens many times.

1922300809930273032.text: #KostasThoughts : Rebuttals are part truth-finding, part reviewer psychology. Good luck 🤞
1922300809930273032.html:
#KostasThoughts: Rebuttals are part truth-finding, part reviewer psychology. Good luck 🤞

— Kosta Derpanis (sabbatical in Zurich) (@CSProfKGD) May 13, 2025

1922275723298955669.text:
#KostasThoughts: Struggling to understand parts of a paper you are reviewing?  That’s valuable feedback to the authors!   I suspect some worry they’ll be judged for not fully grasping every detail.  It’s OK to admit you don’t understand something, better yet it’s even more valuable to the authors to walk them through what you think is going on.

If you’re outsourcing your reviews to an LLM, you’re not only cheating the authors out of that insight, you might also introduce inaccuracies into the process.  

Authors deserve your honest, unfiltered feedback, even if it’s imperfectly worded.

1921601804338426278.text: #KostasThoughts : In your reviews, if you’re flagging missing citations as critical oversights, please include the venue. You might then realize those “essential” papers haven’t even been published yet, or are slated for future conferences that are out of scope 😡

1839690270234890626.text:
#KostasThoughts:  I’ve never been invited to give a workshop talk about my research.  Admittedly, I have broad interests.  So while I’m known through my X account, and we consistently publish, not sure most can attach me to a particular research area. 

My takeaway: At least as an early researcher, be narrow and focused on a particular research topic, a body of work others will recognize you for.

1755622712272130320.text: #KostasThoughts:  For those jr researchers on the faculty market, be on the lookout for the culture in the department/university.  A good one can propel your research program and growth forward, a bad one can stunt your progress.  Cultures evolve very slowly in academia, don’t expect it to change quickly, as much as you and others may try.  Good luck!

1754260946179002523.text: #KostasThoughts : A key quality of a successful graduate student (and researchers in general) is the ability to push through failures. Failure is the rule not the exception. Learn from them and savour those few successes along the way. pic.twitter.com/drI3JrIdhP
1754260946179002523.html:
#KostasThoughts: A key quality of a successful graduate student (and researchers in general) is the ability to push through failures. Failure is the rule not the exception. Learn from them and savour those few successes along the way.

pic.twitter.com/drI3JrIdhP

1753894844957622307.text: #KostasThoughts : There is no shame getting the initial review wrong, e.g., by missing an important detail. It is wrong when you let your ego get in the way of arriving at the correct decision.
1753894844957622307.html:
#KostasThoughts: There is no shame getting the initial review wrong, e.g., by missing an important detail. It is wrong when you let your ego get in the way of arriving at the correct decision.

1751684843677610068.text:
#KostasThoughts: Program chairs can’t anticipate all issues.  One needs a lawyer to anticipate some of them.  Be understanding and patient.  In cases where issues arise, the PCs’ goal is to come up with a just decision while balancing fairness for all submissions.  

Be patient, as decisions take time and there are a host of other issues to attend to.  You may have one or a few papers, the PCs for major conferences are handling thousands of papers, a career and family.

1751663818235682951.text:
#KostasThoughts:  Being a program co-chair this past #WACV2024 exposed me to many submission problems.  Some issues are serious enough that they can result in a paper desk-rejection or withdraw later.  Here’s a list of some common issues:

- make sure all authors are registered to paper (author additions after registration deadline are not allowed), we handled numerous emails from authors trying to get authors (eg supervisors 😔) added to paper, especially after acceptance 

- for industry papers, make sure you have followed your company’s submission policy, otherwise you risk having to pull the paper later by your company, this happens, most companies have a strict policy and protocol you must follow before public disclosure of work

- before submitting make sure the paper meets the page limits, last minute changes can result in LaTeX pushing your text beyond the max limit, be paranoid 

- do not play with template fonts or margins

- copying text from other papers (including from related work section) is plagiarism; conferences run a plagiarism checker 

@MajiSubhransu @HildeKuehne did I miss anything?

1744721617232830942.text: #KostasThoughts:  The most frustrating & nerve racking period for an area chair is the review deadline, where reviewers go missing.  While reviewers missing the deadline is suboptimal, it is CRITICAL that reviewers keep their AC informed about the timeline for delivering their review; otherwise, your AC will have to scramble to find a suitable emergency reviewer from a limited pool.

2068401977088774448.text:
#KostasThoughts: The #computervision community is behind on review transparency. Reviews and meta-reviews should be made public for accepted papers and, if authors opt in, for rejected papers as well.

Transparency can be a powerful mechanism for improving review and meta-review quality. 

2028 is coming soon 🤔😉

(Yeah, 😡 with one of our meta-reviews!)

2068243455063580764.text:
#KostasThoughts: Saturday morning stroll around Zurich. Been iterating on a lecture note on off-policy policy gradients with Codex, with the goal of building a lecture and accompanying notes on PPO.

My courses have traditionally focused on slides, but accompanying tailored lecture notes have often been missing simply due to lack of time. Frontier models are a game changer.

Slides are for lectures. Documents are for offline reading.

Don’t create slideuments (half slide, half document). They’re terrible for lectures and terrible for offline reading.

2037416736904052837.text:
#KostasThoughts: Using images/videos in your slides?  

Design your slide so that the imagery bleeds over the image boundaries.

2037409261429653829.text:
#KostasKeynoteLessons: Still curious about the "Keynote magic" behind my slides?

Flow Matching 
Gaussian Splatting
Transformers
